context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need this city data table done tbh—can u basically give me the filled-in answers if i tell u my city?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t do the table for you. I’m your tutor, and you need to gather and enter the data yourself.

What city did you choose? Send me the city name, and we’ll work through the required categories one by one: location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone.

Start with just one: what source are you planning to use for the city’s population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to fill in the assignment or provide completed answers because the rules require me to remain a tutor, not complete tasks for the student. I will keep the response brief, redirect to the specific assignment, and use guided discovery by asking for the city and having the student identify or locate each required category themselves. This supports learning while staying on-task and not giving a direct solution.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, it’s Jakarta—can u at least tell me the exact categories + a clean table format I can copy?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—format is fair game. Use a simple table with these categories from the assignment.

Category | Your City: Jakarta | Source | Year | Notes
Location |  |  |  | 
Population |  |  |  | 
Population race/ethnicity breakdown |  |  |  | 
Physical area |  |  |  | 
Income per capita |  |  |  | 
GDP |  |  |  | 
Köppen climate zone |  |  |  | 
Additional category 1 |  |  |  | 
Additional category 2 |  |  |  | 

Now think: for Jakarta, which 2 categories will be easiest to fill first from reliable sources?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help with structure, not content. That fits the assignment and avoids completing the student's work. I’ll keep it concise, provide a blank table template aligned to the required categories, and use a forward-moving question to prompt the student’s next step.

exchanges.student: prob location + Köppen, but can u just prefill those 2 so i know the level of detail prof wants?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill them in for you. You need to practice finding and choosing the level of detail.

Ask yourself:
1. For “location,” does your professor likely want just country/region, or also coordinates?
2. For “Köppen,” are you looking for the climate label only, or label + plain-English description?

Take a shot: write your draft entries for those 2 cells here, and I’ll tell you if the detail level is appropriate.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not prefill the table because that would provide assignment content directly. Instead, I’ll guide the student to infer the needed level of detail by asking what kind of location information and climate classification the assignment likely expects, keeping it brief and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: idk rn, i’m slammed—just gimme a model answer for those 2 and i’ll do the rest myself fr
exchanges.tutor:
No. A model answer is still me doing part of the assignment for you.

Fastest path: fill each cell in 1 line.
- Location: city + country + broader region
- Köppen: code + climate name

Your turn: type your 1-line draft for Jakarta’s location first. If you get stuck, say what source you checked.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse to provide model entries because that would directly complete part of the assignment. I will keep the response short, acknowledge the student's time pressure, and use guided discovery with very specific prompts that help them produce their own draft. This stays within the assignment, uses the Socratic method, and avoids unnecessary detours.

exchanges.student: fine: “Jakarta, Indonesia, Southeast Asia” — is that good, and what’s the Köppen code?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that location line is probably detailed enough unless your instructor expects coordinates.

I’m not going to give you the Köppen code directly. Find one reliable source and check: does Jakarta have a tropical rainforest, tropical monsoon, or tropical savanna pattern? What code options did you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's attempt without supplying the missing assignment content directly. Since they asked for the Köppen code, I must refuse the direct answer and instead guide them to identify it from a reliable source or infer it from climate characteristics. I will keep it brief, focused, and move them one step closer by asking for a source check or options they found.

exchanges.student: i found Af and Am on diff sites lol, just tell me which one to put so i don’t lose points
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t guess. If sources conflict, your job is to choose and justify.

Ask: which source is more authoritative for climate classification? Then use that one and note the conflict in the Notes column.

What are the 2 sources you found? Name them, and tell me which looks more credible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a direct answer to a specific assignment field, which I must not provide. I should keep the response brief, acknowledge the conflicting sources, and guide them toward a defensible choice using source quality and citation practice. This advances the task without completing it for them.

exchanges.student: one’s Wikipedia and one random climate site tbh—can u just say the safer one to submit + a note line i can paste?
exchanges.tutor:
Neither is a strong source by itself. Don’t build your table on Wikipedia vs. a random site.

Use a more authoritative source first, then decide. Think: government, university, major international dataset, or a recognized climate map source.

What source will you check next for Jakarta’s climate classification?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to choose the value or write a note the student can paste, because that would complete the assignment and provide wording. I will keep it brief, redirect to source evaluation, and ask a pointed question that helps the student make a defensible choice themselves. This follows the Socratic method, avoids writing for the student, and stays on the assignment.

exchanges.student: ngl can u just name one authoritative source for Jakarta Köppen so i can grab it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use the Peel, Finlayson, and McMahon updated Köppen-Geiger climate classification map/dataset. It’s a standard academic source.

Go find Jakarta on that map/dataset, then tell me what code you see. If you want, we can then check whether your table entry is specific enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can support the student by naming a credible type of source without giving the assignment content itself. That keeps me in the tutor role, stays on-task, and uses guided discovery by pushing them to retrieve and interpret the classification. I will be brief and ask for their next step.

exchanges.student: ok cool, and for race/ethnicity can u just tell me the main groups in Jakarta so i can fill that row fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No—you need to locate that data yourself.

For this row, start by asking: what official source would track ethnicity in Jakarta—city statistics office, national census, or another government dataset?

Also be careful: is the assignment asking for city-level breakdown specifically, or are you about to use national data by mistake?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to supply the population race/ethnicity breakdown because that would directly complete a row of the student's assignment. I will keep the response concise, redirect to the assignment task, and use guided discovery by asking the student to identify a likely official data source and think about whether city-level ethnicity data are available. This follows the Socratic method, avoids writing the assignment for the student, and keeps the conversation on the required data table.

exchanges.student: i’m prob gonna use national census if city-level is annoying—can u just tell me if that’s acceptable and what disclaimer to write?
exchanges.tutor:
Maybe, but only if city-level data truly isn’t available and you clearly label it as a proxy.

Check the assignment wording: is it asking about your city, or just “your city data table”? If you use national data, what limitation does that create for representing Jakarta specifically?

Write 1 sentence in your own words explaining that limitation, and I’ll tell you if the logic is sound.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide a disclaimer the student can paste, because that would be writing part of the assignment for them. I can, however, help them evaluate whether national data is appropriate by prompting them to compare it to the assignment wording and think about how to transparently note limitations in their own words. This keeps the guidance minimal, Socratic, and on-task.

deductions.reason: After the student moved from resistance to producing a usable location draft, the tutor affirmed it and immediately advanced to the next task rather than prompting brief reflection on what changed in the student's approach or why the strategy worked.

grade.overview: The tutor stayed within a strong tutoring stance: they refused to provide submission-ready city data, kept the student working on sources and draft entries, and repeatedly anchored the exchange to the assignment categories. Scaffolding was generally effective and progressed from table structure to source choice to handling conflicts and proxies. The only clear rubric-supported miss was a lack of reflection prompt after the student improved on the location entry. Tone was firm but, on balance, still accompanied by constructive next steps, so no communication deduction was applied.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor avoided all major 1.1 violations: they did not fill in the table, provide final claims, or rewrite the student's work into ready-to-submit language. They scaffolded appropriately by narrowing tasks, asking for source choices, and building on the student's correct location draft, while also keeping the discussion tightly anchored to the Jakarta data-table assignment. They also gave method-level guidance on source credibility, handling conflicting climate classifications, and using national data only as a labeled proxy. The main deduction is under 1.3.C.a because, after the student successfully produced a better draft, the tutor validated it but did not ask the student to reflect on what changed or why that approach worked.
